title: Hardware wallet is a tool, not a plan
body:
A hardware wallet is useful.

It is not a plan.

The plan is:
who signs
where backups live
who knows what
what happens if you die
what happens if one device fails
what happens if one person panics

The tool matters.
The system matters more.

title: USDT vs Bitcoin
body:
USDT on Tron is cheap dollar movement.

Bitcoin is hard money exit.

Both can be useful.
They are not the same thing.

Confusing rails with money is how people fool themselves into thinking they're hedged when they're just holding the same currency on a different wire.

title: Seed in a drawer is not inheritance
body:
A seed phrase in a drawer can protect you from a broken device.

It does not protect your family from confusion.

Inheritance is not just access.
It is instructions, authority, timing, and trust.

If your spouse can't act on it without a private detective, it isn't a plan.

title: Why I build BSA in public
body:
If a Bitcoin lesson saves you from one bad send, one fake wallet, one lost backup, or one inheritance disaster, it probably paid for itself.

That's why I'm building Bitcoin Sovereign Academy in public.

Free to learn.
Value for value if useful.

title: What a seed phrase actually is
body:
A seed phrase is not a password.

It is the wallet.

If 12 or 24 words exist in the right order, your bitcoin can be reconstructed on any compatible device, anywhere, by anyone.

That's the power. And the warning.

Full lesson: https://bitcoinsovereign.academy/start/

title: A UTXO is just a receipt
body:
A UTXO is a receipt that says "this amount went to this address and hasn't been spent yet."

Your balance is just the sum of your unspent receipts.

There is no account. There is no balance field. There is a stack of receipts the network agrees you haven't used.

title: Untested backup
body:
A backup you never tested is just a hope with better packaging.

Once a year, on a date you'll remember, restore a wallet from your seed onto a clean device. Match the first receive address. Then wipe it.

That's the whole drill.

The families who lose Bitcoin almost never skipped the backup. They skipped the test.

title: Money is the unit you measure your life in
body:
Money is not just what you spend.

It is what you measure your life in.

Rent in dollars. Salary in dollars. Time in dollar-hours.

If the unit keeps changing, the plan keeps lying.

title: Five questions that test your custody
body:
Can your heirs recover it?

Can you send safely without copy-pasting an address you didn't verify?

Can you survive losing your phone?

Can you restore without one person becoming the single point of failure?

Can you explain it to your spouse in 10 minutes?

If any answer is "no," that's the next lesson.

title: Exposure vs control
body:
An ETF gives exposure.

Self custody gives control.

Collaborative custody reduces single points of failure.

Three different products. Three different problems they solve.

Clients confused about which one they need will buy the wrong one and call it Bitcoin.

title: Spending wallet ≠ savings wallet
body:
A spending wallet is not a savings wallet.

A savings wallet is not a spending wallet.

Mixing them is how people learn the word "address reuse" the expensive way.

Keep small on hot. Keep most on cold. Don't move the line under pressure.

title: What self custody actually means
body:
Self custody isn't a slogan.

It means you hold the keys. Not a company. Not an app. Not a help desk.

With that comes the upside: no one can freeze, censor, or lose your money for you.

And the downside: no one can recover it for you either.

The upside is worth it only if you build the system for the downside.

title: What Lightning is, in one line
body:
Lightning is fast, cheap payments that settle on Bitcoin underneath when they need to.

Layer 1 is the courthouse. Layer 2 is the lobby.

Most of your day-to-day happens in the lobby. The courthouse is for when something matters enough to make permanent.

title: Why running a node is mostly about questions
body:
A node doesn't earn you sats. It doesn't move faster than your wallet.

What it does: it lets you stop asking other people whether the network is okay.

Is my transaction confirmed? Are these the real rules? Did anyone print extra?

A node is the answer to those questions without trusting the asker.

title: Verify the address
body:
Most stolen Bitcoin in 2026 wasn't hacked.

It was sent to a swapped address.

Malware changes one character in your clipboard. Phishing changes the whole receiver. AI-generated invoice emails look indistinguishable from real ones.

Verify the first 4 and last 4 characters of any receiving address on the device that will receive it. Every time.

title: Multisig vs single sig
body:
Single sig is one key. One way to lose it. One way to be coerced.

Multisig is many keys, many places, many people.

More moving parts. More planning. Less catastrophic failure mode.

For amounts that matter to a family, multisig is the answer to "what if one of us is unavailable, compromised, or gone."

title: The unopened letter
body:
Most inheritance plans for Bitcoin are an envelope no one knows exists, in a drawer no one will open, with a phrase no one understands.

A real plan has:
— at least one person who knows it exists
— a separate person who knows how to use it
— a third party (advisor, attorney) who can confirm authority

A secret with no chain of custody is not a plan.

title: Inheritance is where most Bitcoin advice quietly breaks
body:
Most Bitcoin advice ends at "buy and self-custody."

It never makes it to "and here is what happens when you're not the one holding the keys."

That's the part that breaks families.

If you or your clients need that part written down: https://meetings.hubspot.com/dalia-platt

title: Tell one person
body:
The single most underrated inheritance step:

Tell one trusted person that bitcoin exists, roughly how much, and who to call if something happens to you.

Not the seed. Not the keys. Just the existence.

Many families have lost six figures because no one ever knew there was anything to look for.

title: Money vs payment rail
body:
A dollar is money. PayPal is a rail. Tron is a rail. Lightning is a rail.

Bitcoin is both: money and a rail.

Most of the public debate is people confusing the two. Once you separate them, half the arguments stop mattering.

title: Stablecoins are useful and not Bitcoin
body:
In a country with capital controls, a stablecoin can save your savings this week.

It cannot save your savings over twenty years.

Different timeframe. Different problem. Different tool.

Use them for what each is good at. Don't confuse them.

title: Lose your phone test
body:
Pick a random Saturday. Pretend your phone is gone.

Can you still access your Bitcoin?
Can you still send?
Can you prove you own it?

If the answer requires a particular app store account, a particular SIM, and a particular cloud login — that's the fragility you didn't see.

title: Practice without risking real Bitcoin
body:
Most people learn Bitcoin custody by losing money once.

There's a cheaper way: practice with the interactive demos. Send a transaction, build a multisig, recover from a seed — without ever touching real funds.

https://bitcoinsovereign.academy/interactive-demos/

title: What an ETF actually solves
body:
An ETF solves access, exposure, and tax reporting.

It does not solve seizure risk, counterparty risk, or generational transfer.

If your client's reason for holding Bitcoin is "in case the system breaks," an ETF is the wrong product. If their reason is "diversification on a brokerage statement," it's fine.

Different reasons. Different products.

title: Collaborative custody, in one sentence
body:
Collaborative custody means the client holds one key, a professional holds one key, and a third party holds one key — any two can sign.

No single party can move funds alone. No single party can lock the client out.

The right product for clients who want self custody without being a single point of failure for their own family.

title: What advisors keep getting wrong
body:
Three things I keep seeing advisors miss:

1. Treating Bitcoin like a stock when the client wants it like a deed.
2. Pretending an ETF and a wallet solve the same problem.
3. Skipping inheritance because it feels like the client's job.

If your firm needs that gap closed: https://meetings.hubspot.com/dalia-platt

title: Debasement is a haircut, not a clip
body:
Debasement doesn't show up as a tax. It shows up as a slow haircut on the unit your salary is paid in.

It's the difference between "I make more" and "I have more."

If the second one isn't true and the first one is, the unit changed underneath you.

title: Scarcity isn't a marketing point
body:
Scarcity is not what makes Bitcoin valuable to its users.

What scarcity does is make the unit stop lying about itself.

If no one can quietly print more, the number you saved last year still means what it meant. That's the whole point.

Not a moonshot. Not a hedge. A unit you can trust to stay the same shape.

title: What a hardware wallet does
body:
A hardware wallet does one thing well:

It keeps your private keys on a chip that never touches the internet, and only signs transactions when you press a button after reading the destination on its own screen.

That's it.

The rest is the system you build around it.

title: Privacy isn't paranoia
body:
Bitcoin is public.

Every transaction is on a ledger every node can read. "Anonymous" isn't the default; transparent-but-pseudonymous is.

Protecting your privacy isn't paranoia. It's not telling everyone your bank balance.

You wouldn't share your salary with strangers. Don't share your UTXOs with them either.

title: The 'screenshot the seed' mistake
body:
Screenshotting your seed phrase puts it in:

— your phone's photo roll
— your cloud backup
— possibly your laptop's photo sync
— the recovery system of whoever runs that cloud

A single screenshot can be worth more to an attacker than the device the seed was written down on.

title: On the exchange, not yours
body:
If your bitcoin is on an exchange, it's not yours.

Not a slogan. A bookkeeping fact.

The exchange has an IOU to you. They hold the actual UTXOs.

When they pause withdrawals, fail an audit, or freeze accounts, that distinction is the only thing that matters.

title: "I'll set it up later"
body:
The most common Bitcoin custody mistake isn't technical.

It's "I'll set it up properly later."

Months pass. Funds accumulate in the easiest wallet. The plan stays a plan.

The gap between buying Bitcoin and actually custodying it is where most of the losses happen.

title: Steel, not paper
body:
Paper backups are fine until they meet a leak, a flood, a fire, or a curious child.

If the amount is non-trivial, write the seed on steel. Once. Verify. Store.

It's a one-time cost that converts "hope" into "infrastructure."

title: The passphrase you forgot is the wallet you lost
body:
A BIP39 passphrase adds a 25th word only you know.

If you forget it, the funds are gone. There is no "reset."

Use one only if you've planned where it's stored, who knows it exists, and how it gets to your heirs without you.

Otherwise you've added a single point of failure shaped like security.

title: Four questions for an inheritance plan
body:
A real Bitcoin inheritance plan answers:

1. Who knows it exists?
2. Who knows how to access it?
3. What proves they have the right to?
4. What happens if any one of them is unavailable?

If you can't answer all four out loud, it's not a plan yet.

title: This week's lesson
body:
This week, the most useful lesson here helped people understand the difference between a wallet and a plan.

Free to read.
Zaps help keep it that way.

Value for value.

title: Worth a zap if useful
body:
If something here saved you confusion, risk, or time this week, a zap closes the loop.

Not a tip jar. A signal.

It tells me which lessons land, which ones miss, and what to build next.

title: What zaps fund
body:
Zaps to this account fund:

— more interactive Bitcoin demos
— more Spanish lessons for LATAM
— more advisor and family-office materials

No employees. No marketing budget. No coin shilling.

If that's the future you want, you already know what to do.

title: Start here if you're new
body:
If you're new and want to skip the noise:

1. Read what money is.
2. Set up a wallet you can lose and replace.
3. Move a small amount you can afford to lose entirely.
4. Then make the plan for the amount you can't.

We sequenced this one for you: https://bitcoinsovereign.academy/start/

title: Empieza por aquí
body:
Si estás empezando y no quieres perderte en el ruido:

1. Entiende qué es el dinero.
2. Arma una billetera que puedas perder y reemplazar.
3. Mueve un monto pequeño que no te cambie la vida si lo pierdes.
4. Después haz el plan para el monto que sí.

Lo ordenamos así para que lo sigas paso a paso:
https://bitcoinsovereign.academy/start/

title: El hardware wallet no es un plan
body:
Un hardware wallet es una herramienta.

No es un plan.

El plan responde:
quién firma
dónde están los respaldos
quién sabe qué
qué pasa si te mueres
qué pasa si falla un dispositivo
qué pasa si alguien entra en pánico

La herramienta importa.
El sistema importa más.

title: Cuéntale a una persona
body:
El paso de herencia más subestimado:

Cuéntale a una persona de confianza que tienes bitcoin, más o menos cuánto, y a quién deberían llamar si algo te pasa.

No la semilla. No las llaves. Solo la existencia.

Muchas familias han perdido cifras de seis dígitos porque nadie supo que había algo que buscar.

title: USDT no es Bitcoin
body:
USDT en Tron sirve para mover dólares barato.

Bitcoin es dinero duro para salir del sistema.

Las dos cosas pueden ser útiles.
No son lo mismo.

Confundir el riel con el dinero es como creer que te cubres del peso sosteniéndolo en otra red.
